overview.summary: A sophisticated cyber attack orchestrated by 'Operation Dust Storm' targets the Healthcare and Public Health sector. The attackers employ a multi-stage approach, starting with reconnaissance to identify vulnerable points, followed by resource development to establish command and control infrastructure. Initial access is gained through phishing, leading to execution of malware. Persistence is maintained via web shells on compromised servers. Privilege escalation allows deeper infiltration, while defense evasion techniques are employed to avoid detection. Credential access is achieved by stealing credentials from password stores. Discovery and lateral movement enable the attackers to navigate the network effectively. Collection of sensitive data precedes exfiltration over web services. Finally, impact is realized through data encryption demanding ransom.

Scenario.attack_surface: AVAILABLE_SURFACES: Internet-facing assets, Command & Control Server, Application Log, Public IP/Network Block, Logon Session, Network Traffic
Scenario.attack_vector: AVAILABLE_VECTORS: Email, Web, Network Traffic Flow, Response Metadata, Reg.exe Utility, Web Shell, Application Log Content, File Access, Network Service Scanning, Network Traffic Flow, Logon Session Creation, Network Traffic Content, Data Encrypted for Impact

stage.name: Reconnaissance
stage.capec: CAPEC ID: 309 Name: Network Topology Mapping
stage.description: The attackers engage in active scanning activities to map network nodes, hosts, devices, and routes within the healthcare sector's external network.

stage.name: Resource Development
stage.capec: CAPEC ID: 522 Name: Malicious Hardware Component Replacement
stage.description: Establishment of fake domains and servers to host phishing pages and malware, facilitating future communication with compromised systems.

stage.name: Initial Access
stage.description: Attackers send emails with malicious attachments or links to unsuspecting employees, aiming to gain initial access to the network.

stage.name: Execution
stage.capec: CAPEC ID: 635 Name: Alternative Execution Due to Deceptive Filenames
stage.description: Tricking users into executing malware by exploiting human error and deceptive filenames.

stage.name: Persistence
stage.capec: CAPEC ID: 650 Name: Upload a Web Shell to a Web Server
stage.description: Maintaining access through a backdoor on compromised web servers, ensuring continuous presence within the network.

stage.name: Privilege Escalation
stage.description: Gaining higher-level permissions once initial access is established, allowing deeper infiltration into the system.

stage.name: Defense Evasion
stage.capec: CAPEC ID: 578 Name: Disable Security Software
stage.description: Impairing defenses to avoid detection during the attack by disabling security software.

stage.name: Credential Access
stage.capec: CAPEC ID: 645 Name: Use of Captured Tickets (Pass The Ticket)
stage.description: Stealing credentials stored in browsers or keychain applications to gain unauthorized access.

stage.name: Discovery
stage.capec: CAPEC ID: 309 Name: Network Topology Mapping
stage.description: Mapping the network to identify valuable data and systems for further exploitation.

stage.name: Lateral Movement
stage.capec: CAPEC ID: 324 Name: TCP (ISN) Sequence Predictability Probe
stage.description: Using legitimate remote services to move laterally within the network.

stage.name: Collection
stage.capec: CAPEC ID: 277 Name: Data Interchange Protocol Manipulation
stage.description: Gathering sensitive information from databases and repositories to collect valuable data.

stage.name: Exfiltration
stage.description: Transferring stolen data out of the network using web services.

stage.name: Impact
stage.capec: CAPEC ID: 609 Name: Cellular Traffic Intercept
stage.description: Encrypting data to disrupt operations and force ransom payment.

Scenario.summary: Operation Dust Storm employs a complex multistage cyber attack targeting the healthcare and public health sector. Starting with reconnaissance and resource development, the attackers gain initial access through phishing, maintain persistence with web shells, escalate privileges, evade defenses, access credentials, discover valuable assets, move laterally, collect sensitive data, and finally exfiltrate it before impacting the victim by encrypting data and demanding ransom.
